content.text: ) is the standard loss function for
in neural networks. It measures the difference between two probability distributions—the predicted probabilities and the true labels—and penalizes confident wrong predictions severely.
Problem: Classify image as cat, dog, or bird (3 classes)
Moderate loss (uncertain but correct class has highest probability).
Severe penalty when prediction is confident but incorrect
- Measures "surprise" of the prediction - Low probability → large negative log → high surprise - High probability → small negative log → low surprise
- Makes loss positive (we want to minimize) - Converts "surprise" to loss
you need to encode the true distribution using your predicted distribution.
: Average bits needed to encode events from true distribution
: Average bits needed if you use predicted distribution instead
: Extra bits wasted due to using wrong distribution
Why Cross-Entropy is Better Than MSE for Classification
If you use MSE for classification with a prediction of 0.01 for the correct class:
Cross-entropy penalizes this confidently wrong prediction
More importantly, MSE's gradient is weak when predictions are wrong, causing slow training.
The gradient is crucial for training and is remarkably simple:
= true label (1 for correct class, 0 others)
The gradient is clean and direct
combination is the standard for multi-class classification.
Same simple form — no vanishing gradient problem.
Confidently wrong predictions get large gradients → fast correction
Works well with sigmoid/softmax — avoids gradient saturation
Established best practice for all classification problems
Rewards confident correct predictions, punishes confident wrong ones
If labels are incorrect, large penalties can destabilize training
Output must be in range (use sigmoid/softmax)
log(0) is undefined — use log-softmax trick for stability
Standard for spam detection, yes/no questions
LLMs predict next token from vocabulary
Universal standard for all classification tasks
Cross-Entropy in Generative AI: LLM Next-Token Prediction
In language models (GPT, BERT, etc.), cross-entropy measures how well the model predicts the next token:
: Raw logits for each token in vocabulary
: Compare predicted vs. true next token
is very small can cause numerical issues (log(0) = undefined)
This keeps values in stable range. Most libraries implement this automatically.
Cross-Entropy vs MSE: Quick Comparison Table
: Severe penalty when prediction is confident but incorrect
: Large gradients when wrong → fast learning
: Cross-entropy for classification, MSE for regression
: Measures extra bits needed to encode true distribution using prediction
: Standard loss for next-token prediction in all Transformers
: Use log-softmax trick to prevent log(0) issues
Cross-entropy is the foundation loss function for classification in neural networks and is essential for understanding training objectives in Generative AI models like LLMs.
